coredata.dc:title: One-pot fabrication of boric acid assisted N-doped CDs and advanced fluorescence sensing property from yellow to red for Fe3+ in acidic water environment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zengchen
Wang, Zimin
Zhang, Wenbo
Xu, Kaibo
Zhu, Wenping
Chen, Hongyu
Chen, Yahong
Li, Yanxia
Wang, Baodui
coredata.dc:description: Iron is one very important element, which plays key roles in living organisms. Especially, ferric iron ions participate in various biochemical processes of the body through functions such as heme synthesis, immune regulation, iron utilization and transport, redox reactions, and gene expression regulation. Herein, a highly selective fluorescence sensor for ferric ion based on boric acid assisted N-doped CDs was prepared. In the sensing process of Fe3+, the fluorescence of BA-N-CDs water solution changes from yellow to red obviously in acidic condition, accompanied by the change in color of the solution from pale yellow to dark red. In addition, the fluorescence sensor shows low detecting limit (nanomolar level) by fluorescence titration spectrum. Moreover, according to evaluate excellent fluorescence imaging of sensor toward Fe3+in M109 cell and tomato leaf, the study provided an important idea for constructing water-soluble fluorescence sensor for Fe3+in organism and plant system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Boric acid
Fluorescence sensor
Cell imaging
Chloroplast imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing Fe3+ in pure water environment without buffer solution
The SEM, HR-TEM, element mapping and XPS analysis of BA-N-CDs
The FT-IR and XRD spectra of BA-N-CDs
Fluorescence selectivity spectra, fluorescence competition spectrum toward various metal ions and inferred fluorescence response mechanism
Fluorescence titration spectrum, UV-VIS titration spectrum, fluorescence changes, color changes, 1HNMR titration and ESI-MS titration of sensor BA-N-CDs with Fe3+
The response time, pH response and paper chromatography sensing activities of sensor BA-N-CDs
Cell fluorescence imaging and fluorescence imaging of plant leaf from sensor BA-N-CDs for sensing Fe3+ in water environment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of BA-N-CDs hybrid compound.
The SEM images of BA-N-CDs and accurate indication of the layered structure.
The HR-TEM images of BA-N-CDs and detail marking of boric acid and NCDs.
The XPS spectrum and characteristic group peaks of BA-N-CDs.
FT-IR (a) and XRD (b) spectra of BA-N-CDs.

a. The fluorescence selectivity spectrum (insert: the water solution fluorescence from BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+), λex = 360 nm, pH = 4.5. b. fluorescence 3D spectra of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+. λex = 360 nm. c. The tyndall effect of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+water solution under laser radiation. d. The inferred fluorescence response mechanism.
The fluorescence titration spectrum (a) and the Uv-vis titration spectrum (b) from sensor BA-N-CDs with Fe3+ in water environment, λex = 360 nm, pH = 4.5.
The paper chromatography sensing process of Fe3+ under natural light and Uv light.
The lung adenocarcinoma cell M109 fluorescence imaging of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+ for blue light excitation (λex = 400 nm).
The tomato plant leaf fluorescence imaging of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+ for blue light excitation (λex = 400 nm). The dot position of the arrow is the chloroplast.
The fluorescence detecting mode toward Fe3+and LOD (limit of detection) from various CDs derived sensor.

row.entry: Nitrogen and Sulfur Codoped Carbon Quantum Dots (N, S-CQD)

head.ce:title: One-pot fabrication of boric acid assisted N-doped CDs and advanced fluorescence sensing property from yellow to red for Fe3+ in acidic water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Eningeering, Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan, Zhoukou Key Laboratory of Nanosensing and Detection, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, Gansu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron is one very important element, which plays key roles in living organisms. Especially, ferric iron ions participate in various biochemical processes of the body through functions such as heme synthesis, immune regulation, iron utilization and transport, redox reactions, and gene expression regulation. Herein, a highly selective fluorescence sensor for ferric ion based on boric acid assisted N-doped CDs was prepared. In the sensing process of Fe3+, the fluorescence of BA-N-CDs water solution changes from yellow to red obviously in acidic condition, accompanied by the change in color of the solution from pale yellow to dark red. In addition, the fluorescence sensor shows low detecting limit (nanomolar level) by fluorescence titration spectrum. Moreover, according to evaluate excellent fluorescence imaging of sensor toward Fe3+in M109 cell and tomato leaf, the study provided an important idea for constructing water-soluble fluorescence sensor for Fe3+in organism and plant system.

A highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ based on carbon dots was synthesized. The sensor exhibited excellent sensing property for Fe3+ accompanied fluorescence change from yellow to red in water environment.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron plays an extremely important role in the body's metabolism and is blood liquid oxygen exchange and transport are important components and participate in a variety of organisms redox system [1–5]. When the iron content in the human body is long-term lack of state, it will cause iron deficiency anemia, consequently resulting in insufficient oxygen delivery in the blood [6–8]. If the oxygen carrying capacity is reduced, it has a serious impact on the aerobic metabolism process of the human body, and causes people to have a lot of symptoms like agrypnia, hypomnesis and nerve tissue damage, et al., similarly, the movement ability of the body is reduced [9–14], In addition, excessive iron in human body will cause hemochromatosis and toxic reactions, but also affect the movement of the human body ability [7,15,16]. Therefore, the appropriate intake of iron is extremely important to living organisms and an appropriate amount of iron is also beneficial to the health of organisms. In the human body, trivalent iron (ferric Fe3+) and bivalent iron (ferrous Fe2+) are the main forms of existence and use. However, due to the presence of oxygen, iron ion mostly exists in the form of trivalent iron. And ferric ions are mainly found in plants, and most iron enters the body in the form of ferric ions, which need to be reduced by iron reductase in intestinal mucosa before absorption. In order to obtain irons, microorganisms have evolved a series of systems capable of chelating iron uptake, and these systems play an important regulatory role in the release of microbial toxic factors and the formation of biofilms [17–19]

In biological systems, iron ions not only have good coordination ability, but also exhibits certain biological activity. Recently, ferroptosis research is a research hot spot and focus of disease research in recent years. ferroptosis is closely related to the pathophysiological processes of various diseases such as tumors and nervous system diseases [20–22].

The iron in the cell is mainly exchanged by the transporter containing trivalent iron, which is transported into the cell through the transferrin receptor TFR on the cell membrane. The acidic pH environment in the cell resulted in free trivalent iron, which is reduced to bivalent iron by iron reductase STEAP3. Ferrous ions in the cell react with peroxides to form ferric ions and peroxy radicals. The resulting peroxy radicals attack lipid molecules and oxidize them to lipid peroxides. In normal cells, lipid peroxides are in homeostasis because the concentration of iron ions remains at a certain level. When the iron ions in the cell are suddenly increased, the fenton reaction will be greatly intensified, and the lipid peroxide will accumulate excessively, resulting in iron death of the cell [23–25]. In addition, some iron ions derived nano drugs like Fe3+-gallotannic acid (TA)-sorafenib (SRF) self-assembled nanosystems (SRF@Fe3+TA) have been utilized to undergo cancer treatment [26–30]. So the real-time detection of ferric ions in human body has very important biological significance.

Current methods like atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic emission spectroscopy (AES), spectral color method (SCM), et al. for detecting iron ions require expensive instruments and complex sample pre-treatment processes. And the selectivity from the detection methods for Fe3+are not ideal, so the fluorescence sensor technology with high selectivity and sensitivity has attracted enough attention. Furthermore, some Fe3+ fluorescence sensor based organic small molecule has been synthesized [31–33]. But the toxicity and water solubility have always troubled the use value of probes. To date, some fluorescence sensors based on nanomaterial system have been studied systemically [34–36]. As is well-known, carbon quantum dots, also known as carbon dots (CDs), are an important class of zero-dimensional carbon materials. Due to some special optical properties, catalytic performance, electrical properties and biocompatibility et al., CDs has aroused very wide attention. Based on excellent tunable optical properties from CDs, it shows broad application prospect in bioimaging, ion recognition and photodynamic therapy. Especially owing to regulable functional groups like -OH, -COOH and -NH2, et al. on the surface of CDs, CDs has some unique luminescence advantages in the process of binding with various ions. As reported in literature, CDs nanoparticles have been used as highly selective fluorescence sensor for some ions like F-, Al3+, Cu2+ and Fe3+, et al. To date, several Fe3+fluorescence sensor based on CDs have been fabricated. However, due to the strong paramagnetism of Fe3+, the sensing process are mainly from fluorescence quenching process of CDs, and other competing ions can disturb the sensing activities. Additionally, lack of support system independence CDs system presents the difficulty of separation. So it is of great practical significance to construct easily separable composite CDs system and realize highly selective fluorescence recognition of iron ions in water environment. However, the surface functionalization by anchoring boric onto the surface of CDs has received little attention, and the sensing ability as well as selectivity of boric acid-functionalized CDs toward metal ion sensing has rarely been reported [37–39].

Herein, by one-pot hydrothermal reaction, nitrogen doped CDs nanoparticles bounded by boric acid (BA-N-CDs) was prepared and separated naturally. On the basis of systematic characterization, the unfamiliar hybrid structure from BA-N-CDs was confirmed, simultaneously, the fluorescence sensing properties of BA-N-CDs toward various metal ions was demonstrated. It is very interesting to note that BA-N-CDs can recognize Fe3+with sensitive fluorescence change from yellow to red in acidic water environment. In addition, the detection limit of sensor BA-N-CDs is found to be 2.45 nM, which provides a highly sensitive method to the functionalized BA-N-CDs for biomedical sensing of Fe3+. Moreover, in the physical environments (animal cell and plant cell), BA-N-CDs can monitor Fe3+ in real time, which greatly expands the scope of sensor application.

Boric acid, urea, o-phenylenediamine and Fe(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Cd(NO3)2 Mg(NO3)2 are purchased from Aladdin corporation Shanghai, China. All the chemicals containing solvents are of reagent grade and are used without further purification.

FT-IR spectrum was obtained by bruker INVENIO instrument. The Uv-vis spectra are from on Perkin-Elmer Lambda-35 Uv-vis spectrophotometer. XRD was tested by D8 FOCUS instrument. HR-ESI-MS was tested by micro TOF-QII10410 instrument. 1HNMR spectra are obtained by Varian VR500-MHz spectrometer with TMS as an internal standard. Fluorescence spectra are analyzed on Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer. The SEM images and element mapping was from JEOL JSM-IT800 field emission scanning electron microscopy. The HR-TEM and element mapping are tested by JEOL JEM-2100 PLUS electron microscope. The XPS were obtained by thermo kalpha spectrometers. Lung adenocarcinoma cell M109 and tomato leaf imaging were performed using Carl Zeiss LSM900 laser confocal fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Fe3+ in pure water environment without buffer solution
ce:section.ce:para: All spectroscopic analysis were measured in pure water solution without buffer solution. Because the boric acid created an acidic microenvironment (pH < 4.5), which could inhibit the hydrolysis of iron ion effectively. In fluorescence sensing process, 2 mL aqueous solution containing 100 μL solution of BA-N-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) was filled in a quartz optical cell. 10 μL Fe3+ solution (1.0 × 10–3 M) was added to above solution with a micro-pipette. The spectra signal was recorded after addition Fe3+ into above solution for 3 s. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrum was 360 nm.

The limit of detection (LOD) sensor BA-N-CDs for Fe3+ in water environment was calculated by the equation [40,41].
L O D =
3 σ
S

Where σ was the standard deviation of a blank signal (BA-N-CDs solution, n = 8), s was the sensitivity (slope of linear plot) of fluorescence sensing for analyzing Fe3+ at 568 nm. LOD was the detection limit. By detailed calculation, the detection limit of sensor BA-N-CDs for Fe3+ was obtained.
The synthesis process of BA-N-CDs was shown in Scheme 1 . BA-N-CDs was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal decomposition reaction and room-temperature natural crystallization.

2 g boric acid, 1 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.2 g urea were dispersed in 20 mL two-distilled water. By a heated process, the mixture gradually dissolved in water. then the solution was transferred to 100 mL high pressure reactor, and the mixed solution was heated for 8 h at 180 °C. After natural cooling, the reaction product was filtered to remove the reaction residues with producing a clear solution. The clear solution was continuously heated at 90 °C to volatilize excessive water solvent, and stop heating when half of the water solvent is reached (10 mL). Under room temperature, brown crystal precipitated naturally from rest water solution. By vacuum drying, 2.35 g target compound BA-N-CDs was obtained (Yield: 73.43 %). Furthermore, it needed to be characterized by various methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM, HR-TEM, element mapping and XPS analysis of BA-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The basic morphology of BA-N-CDs was confirmed by SEM images (Figure), As shown in Fig. 1 , BA-N-CDs exhibited uniform blocky shape. And observed carefully the morphology of BA-N-CDs, we found the blocky material showed lamellar microstructure, which was deduced from the boric acid matrix structure. The lamellar microstructure could anchor NCDs efficiently according to interlamellar hydrogen bond force from. In addition, by element mapping images (Figure S1), we obtained the elements distribution of BA-N-CDs. As shown in Figure S, B, N, C and O elements evenly distributed in the lamellar system. Especially, C element and N element was strong proof of the existence of nitrogen doped carbon points (NCDs). But owing to the excessively small NCDs particles, it could not be observed clearly. So sensor BA-N-CDs needed be characterized and confirmed by HR-TEM images.

HR-TEM images could reveal the refined structure of BA-N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , abundant NCDs nanoparticles were surrounded and covered by boric acid molecules, which showed a certain MOF-like structure (polyhedral configuration) and also indicated that the composite structure of boric acid and NCDs had already formed successfully. We concluded that the peculiar hybrid structure was from the hydrogen bonds interaction between boric acid and NCDs. In addition, we observed the lattice structure of NCDs from BA-N-CDs, and the lattice distance (d = 0.21 nm) was in accord with reported characteristic structure of carbon dots. Moreover, the HR-TEM element mapping images demonstrated the existence of C, N, O and B elements in fining microstructure (Figure S2). All the results based on SEM and HR-TEM indicated that BA-N-CDs hybrid structure was fabricated successfully and its microstructure was confirmed preliminarily.

BA-N-CDs needed be characterized and confirmed by XPS spectra, which could provide rich information about elemental composition and bonding states of BA-N-CDs. Firstly, auger electron elements spectroscopy provided some clear evidences about C (CKL1) and O (OKL1 and OKL2) elements in the surface of BA-N-CDs (Fig. 3
a). The XPS spectrum confirmed the existence of C (1S), N (1S), O (1S) and B (1S) elements. The high resolution peaks of C 1 s (Fig. 3 c) was deconvoluted to three individual peaks at 287.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 284.7 eV, which was assigned to CO, CN/CO and CC/CH, respectively. Especially, the relatively high intensity of the CC/CC peak at 284.7 eV corresponding to graphite-like sp2 C indicated that most of the C atoms in BA-N-CDs were still arranged in a conjugated honeycomb lattice structure. Fig. 3 b showed the morphexistence of B-O group (193 ev), which demonstrated only one boron element state occurred in BA-N-CDs. Compared the XPS bands state of oxygen element, two functional groups (OC and O-B) were confirmed (Fig. 3 d), and by the OC group analysis (533.5 ev), we concluded that it was from carboxyl surface function in the process of NCDs formation. In addition, based on the raw materials of o-phenylenediamine, the CN group (399.5 ev) was also proved to be present in BA-N-CDs. All the results from XPS indicated the successful fabrication of hybrid BA-N-CDs material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FT-IR and XRD spectra of BA-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The FT-IR spectrum of BA-N-CDs was shown in Fig. 4
a. Comparing with the FT-IR o-phenylenediamine and boric acid (Figure S3), the characteristic peaks of BA-N-CDs exhibited significant differences. The absorption peak at 3178.91 cm-1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH, and it changed (46 nm red shift) obviously compared with vibration peak (3226 cm-1) of boric acid. We concluded that the significant redshift was from the weak interaction between -OH and some functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NH2) of carbon dots. In addition, the characteristic band at 1629.54 cm-1 was inferred as the absorption peak of the carbonyl group (-CO), which exhibited excellent consistency with the C1s bands from XPS spectrum. And the FT-IR spectra demonstrated successful fabrication of carbon dots and BA-N-CDs. Furthermore, XRD spectra was also tested to confirm the composition of BA-N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the XRD pattern of BA-N-CDs indicated a broad diffraction band at around 2θ = 21.5°, signifying a nearly identical (002) layer spacing of carbon dots. The sharp XRD peaks at 2θ = 28.5° was attributed to be the characteristic crystalline phase from lamellar configuration of boric acid matrix. According to FT-IR and XRD spectra, the hybrid structure of BA-N-CDs was confirmed. Furthermore, the sensing properties toward various metal ions of BA-N-CDs need be studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity spectra, fluorescence competition spectrum toward various metal ions and inferred fluorescence response mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the fluorescence selectivity of sensor BA-N-CDs toward Fe3+ in acidic water environment (pH = 4.5), the fluorescence selectivity spectrum of BA-N-CDs with various metals ion (Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+) were confirmed. As Shown in Fig. 5
a and Figure S4a, with the addition of various metal ions, only Fe3+ could cause obvious fluorescence change of BA-N-CDs. The yellow fluorescence (560 nm) was quenched intensively and exhibited a red shift to 592 nm (red fluorescence), the 3D fluorescence spectra (BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+) also provided direct evidence emissive wavelength red shift (Fig. 5 b). Moreover, the apparent fluorescence images of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+ under Uv light showed more intuitive fluorescence sensing properties. Upon addition of Fe3+ in water environment, the yellow fluorescence of BA-N-CDs converted to strong red fluorescence. The fluorescence selectivity experiment demonstrated that BA-N-CDs could be used as an effective Fe3+ fluorescence sensor in water environment. Moreover, the water solution of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+ showing a clear tyndall phenomenon (Fig. 5 c). Under the red laser light, the water solution produced bright beams of light, which was from scattering of laser light by nanoparticles. The interesting tyndall effect demonstrated that the size of nanoparticle BA-N-CDs was in the 0–100 nm range, which was less than the wavelength of incident light and confirmed the size consistency of the nanoparticles comparing with the electron microscope data.

Fluorescence competition experiment can evaluate the fluorescent interference capability of other various metal ions toward Fe3+ in acidic water environment. 100 uL Fe3+ion water solution was added to sensor BA-N-CDs water solution, the fluorescence signal was recorded, then equal amount of metal ions water solution (Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+) was injected into the above solution, the fluorescence intensity was confirmed again (Figure S4b). According to compare the fluorescence intensity changes, the competition properties between Fe3+ and other various metal ions were confirmed. As seen in the fluorescence competition spectra, other various metal ions could not disturb the fluorescence changes of BA-N-CDs+Fe3+water solution, which proved the high selectivity of Fe3+fluorescence sensor in water environment again.

The concluded fluorescence sensing mechanism was listed in Fig. 5 d. We deduced that there were abundant surface functional groups like hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups. After addition of Fe3+ in water solution, the intense interaction between surface functional groups and Fe3+ happened. Especially, the excited state electrons partial transfer of carbon nuclei from BA-N-CDs induced by the d orbitals of Fe3+occurred, which could induce changes from yellow to red in the luminescence of carbon nuclei from BA-N-CDs. The fluorescence and sensing mechanism needed be demonstrated by various titration spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence titration spectrum, UV-VIS titration spectrum, fluorescence changes, color changes, 1HNMR titration and ESI-MS titration of sensor BA-N-CDs with Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The Uv-vis titration spectra of sensor BA-N-CDs with Fe3+ in acidic water environment (pH = 4.5) was obtained to confirm the interaction between BA-N-CDs and Fe3+ (Fig. 6 b). Figure showed the Uv-vis spectra shifted significantly. The bands at 248 nm and 290 nm disappeared and merged into one absorption peak at 260 nm. Additionally, the Uv-vis intensity at 260 nm gradually decreased and had a 20 nm shite to 280 nm. We concluded that the bands changes were from the benzene ring absorption changes after the interaction of BA-N-CDs with Fe3+. Moreover, the absorption bands (-CO) at 410 nm exhibited more significant changes, which was with a 40 nm redshift in wavelength and strength rise. The Uv-vis changes at absorption position and strength were very interesting. The Uv-vis changes was also consistent with the apparent color change of BA-N-CDs water solution from faint yellow to deepred. Simultaneously, the fluorescence and color performances of BA-N-CDs water solution with progressively elevated iron ions under Uv light and natural light were highly related to the fluorescence and Uv-vis titration spectra (Figure S6). All the results demonstrated that sensor BA-N-CDs possessed a strong interaction with Fe3+, which induced the fluorescence and color changes of sensor BA-N-CDs.

Additionally, the 1HNMR and ESI-MS titration spectra between sensor BA-N-CDs and Fe3+ ware confirmed to evaluate the interactions between sensor BA-N-CDs and Fe3+. Form 1HNMR titration from BA-N-CDs to BA-N-CDs+Fe3+(Figure S7), we found the NMR characteristic band at 6.75 ppm for BA-N-CDs was obviously passivated, which indicated the interaction force from paramagnetic Fe3+ masked the nuclear magnetic signal. The interactions not only caused the partial transfer of the excited state electrons of BA-N-CDs to the d orbitals of Fe3+, but also lead to the obvious 1HNMR signal changes. Moreover, according to ESI-MS titration (Figure S8), we found ESI-MS after adding Fe3+showed noticeable changes. And by the changes, we concluded that some new molecules like O-phenylenediamine polymer and O-phenylenediamine-boric acid polymer existed in BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs+Fe3+ system. Overall, our results suggested the interesting interaction between BA-N-CDs and Fe3+ in water solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response time, pH response and paper chromatography sensing activities of sensor BA-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The time response on Fe3+ recognition from the sensor BA-N-CDs was checked with various time intervals under optimal pH condition in water environment (Figure S9). It was convincing that the fluorescence intensity of the sensor with Fe3+ reached a stable value after 1.2 s, which indicated that 1.2 S is the optimal response time. Moreover, we concluded that the short response time was very to the benefit of sensing Fe3+ in organism.

The highly selective recognition properties of Fe3+in suitable pH condition by BA-N-CDs sensor was very crucial for sensitive detection. Because the sensing efficiency of the sensor BA-N-CDs for Fe3+ could be influenced by different pH environment. The fluorescence spectra of BA-N-CDs and BA-N-CDs binding with Fe3+ were confirmed within the scope of pH levels. As shown in Figure S10, different pH was made to confirm most ideal pH condition for Fe3+ identification in water environment. BA-N-CDs displayed a strong sensitivity in fluorescence signal response for Fe3+ at a pH range of 1.0∼7.5. So too basic conditions are not conducive to the production of fluorescence from BA-N-CDs. Acidic pH environment was the best conditions for fluorescence generation and Fe3+ sensing. In the system, boric acid provided very suitable acidic condition for Fe3+ recognition, also anchoring NCDs nanoparticles simultaneously.

Paper chromatography is a very simple and portable method for analysis and detection (Fig. 7 ). To date, mass paper chromatography has been prepared, which can detect metal ions, bioactivator and bacteria. et al. According to aqueous infiltration of sensor BA-N-CDs (1.0 mM), Under natural light and Uv light, the color and fluorescence have taken obvious changes. The color was from white to light brown, and the fluorescence changed from blue to yellow. Further, the paper chromatography was dropwise added a low concentration Fe3+ solution (1 uM). In natural light, the filter paper changed to erythrinus, synchronously, the fluorescence showed markedly from yellow to red. The color and fluorescence changes of paper-based chromatography was consistent with the changes of sensor BA-N-CDs water solution which demonstrated that the paper chromatography could detect Fe3+ in water environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell fluorescence imaging and fluorescence imaging of plant leaf from sensor BA-N-CDs for sensing Fe3+ in water environment
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the important role of iron ions in living organisms, the bioluminescence imaging experiment from Fe3+ sensing process could evaluate the fluorescence sensing properties of Fe3+, which extended the application prospect of sensor BA-N-CDs. Because high acidic environment (pH < 6.0) can reduce the chances of cellular survival, so in cell imaging experiment, the pH values was controlled by HEPES buffer solution (pH = 7.0). The lung adenocarcinoma cell M109 was being processed by mature method (Part S1). As shown in Fig. 8 . The lung adenocarcinoma cell M109 was visibly stained and emitted bright yellow fluorescence. And the sensor BA-N-CDs penetrated into cells, which could be labelled clearly. Furthermore, an appropriate solution of iron ions was added to the above solution, the changing cell fluorescent signal was recorded. According to compare the fluorescence change in the cell system, we discovered that the fluorescence signal transferred from yellow to red, and the cell was always labelled clearly. Overall, the highly selective fluorescence sensing property of sensor BA-N-CDs toward Fe3+in organism was confirmed by cell fluorescence imaging.

In conclusion, a novel Fe3+ fluorescence sensor according to hydrothermal method by “fluorescence emissive shift” process was fabricated successfully. In the fluorescence sensor, boric acid containing hydroxyl groups could load nitrogen doped carbon dots and formed stable water-soluble hybrid system (BA-N-CDs). By systemic characterization, we found that BA-N-CDs was uniform macroscopic block layer structure, and BA-N-CDs exhibited similar polyhedral configuration by HR-TEM analysis. In addition, the element mapping from SEM and TEM also confirmed the distribution of B, N, C and O elements. Such the characterization results demonstrated the effective fabrication of composite nanosystem BA-N-CDs. Through fluorescence analysis, BA-N-CDs can identify effectively Fe3+in the interaction process with metal ions under own acidic environment. Especially, BA-N-CDs could distinguish Fe3+ rather than other various metal ions including Fe2+, which showed excellent fluorescence detecting property. More significantly, the fluorescence change from yellow to red occurred with the color change from light yellow to dark red. We concluded that the fluorescence sensing process was mainly from coordination and oxygen features from Fe3+. To enhance the application value of Fe3+ sensor in biological sensing environment, Lung adenocarcinoma cell M109 and tomato plant leaf fluorescence imaging were studied systemically, which demonstrated the fluorescence sensor BA-N-CDs has conveniently tracked Fe3+through fluorescence changes, and the low detection limit are very encouraging. Moreover, by simple paper chromatography, the sensor BA-N-CDs showed highly selective sensing activity for Fe3+, which was a very interesting method for detecting Fe3+. All these results confirmed that BA-N-CDs could be used to detect Fe3+by sensitive fluorescent changes due to convenience and simplicity in water environment, showing the great potential in practical Fe3+ fluorescence sensor application.

Zengchen Liu: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zimin Wang: Methodology. Wenbo Zhang: Methodology. Kaibo Xu: Methodology. Wenping Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Hongyu Chen: Writing – review & editing. Yahong Chen: Writing – review & editing. Yanxia Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Baodui Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Science Foundation of China (21701203). Key Research Projects of Henan Higher Education Institutions (25A150038). Young and Middle-Aged Backbone Teachers of Zhoukou Normal University. Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420396, 242300421636)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2025.142746.
